speaker: interviewer
text: Today is October 23, 1995. The survivor is Mr. Henry Wellner. This is tape two.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, like I mentioned before, we went to [? Shiatin. ?] This was a little town, the border of Romania and Poland. On the other side was Czernowitz. This was our-- we thought that's going to be our destination, to-- to cross over to Czernowitz, and from Czernowitz, to go to Palestine. That time it was Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: But [LAUGHS]-- but we got caught from Jewish militia, from the Jewish boys, communists. They became militia right away. And they told us, hey, you want to go to Palestine? You have to-- you have to stay here, to build a communist-- a communist country. So they put us in jail. At that time, I-- like I-- I told you yesterday, I had a lot of money. And they said, the capitalist, if they find a lot of money, they will send us to Siberia, [INAUDIBLE]. So I took the money and I split on three-- three ways. After they-- and present, they sent us back to Lemberg. The money I never saw again. [LAUGHS] The [INAUDIBLE]-- I split with the other two guys. I never saw again.

speaker: interviewee
text: But coming back to Lemberg, I met a fellow from my town. He knew the family. He knew me. He was surprised that I am here. He said, listen, they think-- they think that you got-- you got killed in you know. Because your father came home without you, and they put up already candles after me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I told myself, I have to go home. I have to go home. And that time, there was a time when the border was open between Russia and Germany. Polish-- Polish territory, where the Russian took over, and the Germany-- and Polish, where the German took over. So I went there, from one train to a other, one train to another. I got home. That was 7 o'clock in the evening. I knew my city very well, so I knew already that everybody was wearing a-- a [NON-ENGLISH], from the people what I met in Lemberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't have any, any documentation, nothing. But I got home. I opened the door. They opened the door for me. Everybody was shocked. I remember, I must be at that time about 16 and 1/2 years old. I saw the life in Lemberg by the Russian. There was already a free-- free-- free for all, a free life. You could do anything if we want to. We could go in the movies. We can-- could go in the-- in the theater. We could do black marketing, you know. Otherwise, we had to do-- we had to make our living.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had a very good impression about-- about the Russian. So I told my parents, listen, I have an idea. Let's all go back-- back. The border was open for two weeks. The border was still open. Let's go back to-- let's go to Lemberg.

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't want to listen to me. But the two brothers-- but the two brothers, my older two brothers-- they packed a few things. And the sister, my sister, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] went with them to the border, to the Russian-- Russian-- Russian-- German-Polish border. She came back. She came back-- they went. They went to Russia. She came back but because she had a boyfriend. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And-- and that's-- she came back. My parents said, where are we going to go. We have a-- the whole family was there. We had everything. We had everything. You know, because my father was connected with the bakers, with the Polish bakers. So we-- food was no-- we had enough food and other-- other-- we had it good. So he didn't want to go no-- no place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Next year, 1940-- am I through with 1939? I think so. So my brothers went to Russia. In 1940, naturally, I had to put on an armband. I used to-- I had to work every day. Where did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the streets. You know, the Germans-- the Germans-- you know, to clean the streets and these things. I don't-- I really don't rem-- don't remember too much. There came an-- there came an order to-- to get together-- they need young people to work on the [GERMAN], on the-- you know, in Germany. And that's going to be only six month, for six months. Such liars-- for six months. So I-- I was the only one. It was a younger brother, but he was too young. I was the only one selected to go for six months.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went for six months. I went. I opened the-- we went about-- I would say about 400 young boys from three cities. We opened the first camp in Klein Mangersdorf. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I stayed there-- I stayed there-- yeah, from 1940 to 1941-- 1941.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the-- when the-- Russian-- German-- German-Russian war broke out, this was in 1941. From there, they sent us to Eichtal, another camp. Because the camps were-- I mean, every 30 kilometers was a camp because we worked-- we built the [GERMAN]. So another camp took over. And you know, we started in forests-- excuse me, we cleaned out the forest. We worked seven days a week. In the forest, we rototill here every day. The [GERMAN] wasn't bad. We still could have packages from home. And 1941, like I said before, my youngest sister got married. And I still got a piece of cake. They sent me still a piece of cake from-- from the wedding. And they-- from there, they sent us to [INAUDIBLE], not a camp. I wasn't in [INAUDIBLE] too long. They needed 300 young men for the Austrian [GERMAN], for the-- because the Russian-- the Russian railroad is 9 centimeter wider than the European. And they needed the-- the railroad for-- for the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they-- they selected 300 [NON-ENGLISH], 300 boys. Each one of us-- I was at that time about 18-- 18 years old. Young. And they sent us to Russia. We didn't go as Jews. We went there as [GERMAN]. Well, they gave us to understand that we are [GERMAN]. That means that we-- we are good. We are better than the other-- than the other Jews. I don't know exactly. And we-- they sent us to Russia. We went from Breslau, from Gross Masselwitz, from our camp, by Breslau-- Gross Masselwitz. We went by-- by train, cattle-- cattle-- you know? And we went to-- through-- we went through Germany, through [? Vindler. ?] You heard about [? Vindler? ?] Who didn't? We saw the-- we saw Jews over there with big, big [NON-ENGLISH] on the back. They work on the railroad too.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came-- we came to Russia. They put us in, in a army camp. It was not a wooden army camp. It was from-- from blocks, from-- [LAUGHS] we're up. We went in there. We felt a smell, a horrible, horrible smell, a dead-- dead smell, of dead people, flesh. And we-- there was-- we had to go in, in a big-- in a big room, a very big room. But the doors were lock-- locked up. We opened the doors. The Jews were not there no more. But [INAUDIBLE], big one [INAUDIBLE] with-- with-- because they-- they sent all the Jews there. They locked up the doors. And the Jews-- Jews died. And the Jews from around the-- around the city, the towns over there-- it was a horrible smell. We cleaned up the place. That was our headquarters.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in the wintertime, 1942. Over there, it was so cold. It was so cold, 40 below zero. And we had to go to work. [LAUGHS] They wanted us to smaller the-- the railroad. They-- how do you-- the train-- the trails, yeah? That's [INAUDIBLE]. And the air-- it was frozen, just plain frozen. If I put in a-- you know, a-- how you call it? We couldn't do nothing. We worked with Russian people, with the young people. We were there, at that time, about 300 of us. A few days later, they got an order to send about 250 closer to the front, to the Russian-- to the Russian front. I was very lucky at that time. I didn't know that I was so lucky. I froze my five fingers. I-- I saw my nose-- my friends told me, look, [INAUDIBLE], my-- I knew it's that bad [INAUDIBLE]. You understand, don't you? No, you don't. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, so I-- I was stupid. I took off the glove and I started to-- you know, with the snow. I wanted to save-- save my nose. My five fingers got frozen. So I wasn't sent any farther. I didn't-- I wasn't sent any farther. The 250 went. Didn't take too long-- about two-- two weeks, maybe three. I don't remember exactly. They started to come back, sick, millions and millions of lice under the skin. But I mean the same thing. You know, in the morning-- well, in the morning, how we washed-- there was like this. And at night, we took off the shirt. We hanged it up on a-- on a tree. In the morning, we just shook the shirt. And you could see on the snow who knows how many lice. And they went in the-- under the skin, already. It was-- then we didn't have-- we didn't have enough to eat. It was cold. We didn't-- we didn't have no place where to wash. We wash with snow. Because we were still young, and you know, that's-- that was the only reason that we survive over there-- over there. But we had a Jewish doctor-- a fine man with the name [? Leidner, ?] Dr. [? Leidner. ?] When the officers-- he was also a doctor for the army over there. And he didn't-- the Germans didn't have a doctor. So he-- he was very well off. They-- here he had plenty of-- he spoke a perfect Germany, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they asked him, listen, why don't the boys go to work? We need to-- we need, you know, people to-- he said, [GERMAN], the boys are sick. They can't do anymore. What are-- what are they sick of? He said, influenza. We had the typhus already. If he would have-- if he would say typhus, that would be the end of everybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: But because we were [GERMAN], a better class of Jews for them, they sent us back, back to Breslau, [SIGHS] also on the cattle train, cattle car-- cars, on a straw.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many did they send back?

speaker: interviewee
text: How many? Mm hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who knows. One-- oh, wait a minute. We came back to Breslau 70 from 300. And on a short time-- two months. You know how we ate in those-- in those-- first of all, after hours and hours of traveling, they opened the doors. So we ran out in snow to get a little bit snow. We-- we ate in the same pot where we relieved ourself. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] So what did we do? We put straw on the pot. We relieved ourself. And through our little window, we throw that away. Anyway, we came after-- after traveling a week or 10 days, on each stop we used to throw out two dead, three dead. In the morning, I woke up, here a dead man, here a frozen man. We came back to Breslau. And they opened-- we came back to Gross Masselwitz, actually. And over there, they opened a special isolation camp for us. I put it down. Will you please take a look? The name of it-- how you call it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, I got sick over there. I got sick of the typhus. And I was-- I was very sick. You know, people with typhus, they go through a crisis. If the crisis OK-- if not, this is it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day, I see a person in front of my-- of my bed. He looks at me. He says, Wellner, what are you-- what are you doing? [SPEAKING POLISH]-- in Polish. What are you doing here? I started to look at him. That was my-- our doctor from the-- from our town, Dr. [? Mittelman. ?] So he knew the whole family very well. He-- he adopted me. If not, I wouldn't be alive. He started to give me a lot of food. I could eat like a horse after-- after a sickness like this. And the boys what didn't have, they died.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day-- he was with us-- I don't remember exactly how long he was with us. Annaberg-- I remember Annaberg. This was the isolation camp. He must have be-- the Germans sent him out from Sosnowiec to help us-- to come back to-- because we were the [GERMAN], because we worked for the front, for the German army. And that time he saw me. So we-- he must have been with-- with us about two months maybe, maybe. I started to come back to my health, to my strength. But the boils, the pus, didn't want to go away. I said, boys-- OK. The boils didn't want to go away. So one time-- one day, he said, listen. Wellner, Wellner-- he called me Wellner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Listen, Wellner. Today is going to come a selection. You'll go to my room and hide over there till the selection's going to be over. Can you imagine? And I-- that's what I did. The selection, they took out, maybe-- who knows, 100, 50, 200 sick people? And they sent them to Auschwitz. So and-- after-- after two months-- that's how long Dr. [? Mittleman ?] was with us. I couldn't wash. I couldn't wash, because the boils had to dry. I was smelling like a pig from far away. And the only thing-- they didn't have no medication. The only thing, it was the sun. The sun healed my-- my wounds. I was walking around with a-- like Tarzan. Walking around. And this helped me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on he went back to [INAUDIBLE] because they needed him over there. And I healed. By the end of '42, I was healed. Still I had all kinds of spots on my hands and my body. But I was healed. They sent me to another camp, [? Vrendig. ?] From there-- from they they sent me-- excuse me-- from there they sent me to Markstadt. Markstadt was in-- in [INAUDIBLE] I think, [INAUDIBLE]. This was a camp of 4,000 boys, Jewish boys-- 4,000-- where everyone worked. On the [NON-ENGLISH], outside of the camp, they had a bunch of POWs, English and French. They started to help us. We did business-- yeah, we did-- we did business with the Polacks. But the Polacks wanted to-- they [NON-ENGLISH] from you, till-- till he gave you anything, nothing. But the-- the PLO, the French, the English, they start to give us cigarettes, piece of chocolate, and this and this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Over there, there was a kapo. Actually, there were more than one kapo. Over there was a kapo. He was from [? Bendin, ?] from the same town. He was-- he was just worst than a SS. He was just-- he was just such a sadist, such a SOB. [NON-ENGLISH]. You-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and he-- I heard that he-- and he had a brother over there. The brother davened every day. And did [NON-ENGLISH] every day, every day. He was from a nice home. That's how-- somehow something's happened to this guy. One day he comes in with another fellow. And he asks the-- the elder-- the elders from this-- from the room, he ask, do you have a guy with the name Wellner? Oh, when I-- my-- my bed was in a corner. When I heard that, I told myself, this is it. This is it. So yeah, I came. I saluted. You had to salute. And they took me out, outside in the room. And he asked me, do you have a sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 21 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a sister with the name [? Topcha? ?] This is-- that's-- I said, yeah. So he took-- he takes out about a thousand mark, German mark and-- and-- and all kind-- and food rations, a bunch. He said-- I ask him, what-- what's going on? He said, you see, your brother is helping my brother in [? Rosner's ?] shop-- in [? Rosner's, ?] Rosner's shop. And she got in touch. She has got in touch with my brother. My brother got in touch with the guy what-- and that's-- and you see? That's how-- she was a [NON-ENGLISH]. She was a [NON-ENGLISH]. That's it? What? No? OK, I can talk? Keep going.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. And at that time, my life started to come back. You know, I didn't-- I didn't have to have the food from the kitchen. I started to buy good food on the outside by the English soldiers, by the French soldiers. And this-- I don't know how long I was in Markstadt. After this, I had a-- I had a cousin. He was the [INAUDIBLE]-- the [GERMAN]. They call him [GERMAN] from camp. So Markstadt wasn't too far from Krottendorf, from the other [GERMAN], where he-- where he was there, the chief, the head of it. And one time he came and he saw me. And he took me to his camp-- me and another nine-- nine boys, 10 of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Krottendorf again-- I was-- I was a big shot over there, because my cousin [NON-ENGLISH]. I got a good place where to work. You know, I work in a place where the Germans, whatever they robbed, whatever they-- they-- stealed in Europe, they send it into a warehouse. The warehouse was on the ground of a-- of a brewery. OK.
